title: The Doubt That Has Arisen in Everyone's Mind!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored; the assessment year, legal chronology, settlement figures and seven-year maximum sentence have been checked against the printed pages; and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The Assistant Commissioner of Income Tax filed a case in the Economic Offences Court at Egmore, Chennai, against former Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and Sasi Enterprises for failing to file income-tax returns for the years from 1991 to 1993. The case has been under trial for seventeen years, since 1997, and has now reached a stage at which it may conclude. I want to explain its details here so that you may understand them.

The case was first filed on 21 August 1997. Jayalalithaa's side said that she had no income during the period identified by the Income Tax Department and had therefore filed no return. The Department did not accept this. Under income-tax law, taxpayers must file a return even for a period in which they have no income.

The case concerning Jayalalithaa and her friend Sasikala's failure to file their income-tax returns for 1993-94 came before Supreme Court Justices B.N. Agrawal and A.K. Mathur on 24 February 2006. Jayalalithaa's counsel sought six weeks' time.
Do you know what the judges then said?

“You are making a mockery of the administration of justice. How much longer can you drag out these court proceedings? You must set an example to others by appearing in court and answering questions under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. What counter-affidavit can you possibly file in this matter? We wish to issue an order today itself directing your client to appear in court and answer the questions put to her.”
The Hindu of 25 February 2006 reported this under the headline “Court pulls up Jayalalithaa”:

“You (Jayalalithaa) are making a mockery of the Judicial Process. How long you can drag the proceedings? You must set an example to others by presenting yourself in the court for questioning under section 313 cr. P.C. What counter affidavit you can file in this matter? We want to pass an order today itself directing your client to be present in the Court.”

On 14 June 2006, the lower court dismissed Jayalalithaa's application seeking discharge from the case. Jayalalithaa and Sasikala had filed petitions seeking discharge but prolonged matters without arguing them, prompting the Income Tax Department to approach the High Court and Supreme Court. On 4 September 2006, the Supreme Court ordered the trial court to decide within four weeks the discharge petitions filed by Jayalalithaa and Sasikala. The Chief Metropolitan Magistrate in Chennai heard and dismissed Jayalalithaa's petition. Thus, nine years after the case was filed in 1997, the accused sought discharge and that petition too was dismissed.

Jayalalithaa's side then appealed to the High Court. That appeal was dismissed on 2 December 2006. She thereafter approached the Supreme Court seeking discharge. On 30 January 2014, the Supreme Court dismissed her appeal. The judges also ordered that Jayalalithaa face the income-tax case and that it be completed within four months.

The judges further stated in their judgment: “Those liable to pay income tax must file their returns properly and without fail. Failure to file is itself an offence. It has become customary for every order issued by the Income Tax Department to be taken on appeal. Details of such appeals are annexed to this case. They make clear the extent to which deliberate efforts have been made to delay this case. Henceforth, all courts must prevent offenders from using the courts to delay cases against them.”

The Supreme Court judgment also said: “The principal issue is whether the burden of proving the charge rests upon the accusing Income Tax Department or upon the accused, Jayalalithaa and Sasikala. In this case, an intention on the part of the accused can be presumed. The accused are therefore obliged to prove beyond doubt that they are innocent. They must establish before the trial court the circumstances in which they failed to file their income-tax returns.” We must therefore remember that the burden of proving their innocence in this case rested on Jayalalithaa and Sasikala.

The case was filed in 1997. The Magistrate's Court dismissed their petition in 2006, nine years later. The High Court dismissed it on 2 December 2006. Eight years after Jayalalithaa appealed in 2006, the Supreme Court dismissed that appeal on 30 January 2014 and ordered the trial completed within four months. Ten months have passed since that Supreme Court order. Has the trial concluded? No.

In this same case, after writing all this and ordering completion within four months, the Supreme Court postponed the trial for three months because Jayalalithaa had to campaign in the parliamentary election. It is worth noting that only four days of campaigning remained at the time. The Court also directed Jayalalithaa and Sasikala to appear without fail on the date fixed by the trial court. Was even that judgment respected? Did they appear without fail on the specified date?

On 20 March 2014, the case came before Judge R. Dakshinamurthy in the First Additional Economic Offences Court at Egmore. Since charges had to be framed, he ordered Jayalalithaa and Sasikala to appear in person on 3 April. They did not. Their lawyer said Jayalalithaa was campaigning and Sasikala suffered from back pain and diabetes, and sought exemption from personal appearance. Counsel for the Income Tax Department strongly opposed the request.

The Magistrate then ordered them to appear on 10 April. They did not appear that day either. When the case was heard on 19 May, neither Jaya nor Sasi appeared. It was adjourned to 3 June. On that date, the judge ordered them to appear on 9 June. Again, they did not. Judge Dakshinamurthy ordered that both must appear in person for trial on 30 June. Was that order implemented? No.

It was Jayalalithaa who disregarded court orders in this manner. When the case was heard on 30 June 2014, a petition filed for Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and Sasi Enterprises said they had applied to the Income Tax Department for departmental consideration of their failure to file returns, that the application remained pending, and that the hearing should therefore be postponed. It was adjourned to 24 July.

There is another fact about this case. As early as 1998, when the BJP led the Union Government and the AIADMK was its ally, efforts were reportedly made by the AIADMK leader to secure release from this income-tax case. On 15 July 1998, The Statesman reported:

“Following the temporary truce Jayalalithaa has reached with the Union Government, the BJP Government has begun taking steps to free her from the income-tax and other cases against her. Commissioner S.C. Jadhav, who supervised the cases against Jayalalithaa, was transferred to what was considered a desirable post in Mumbai. K. Gopalan took his place. P.K. Sridharan, regarded as an officer of integrity, was transferred to another important responsibility, and N.P. Tripathi was appointed in his place. Income-tax officials alleged that such transfers could secure temporary relief for Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and her relatives. By placating Jayalalithaa, the BJP Government obtained the support it needed. In return, they temporarily suspended their demand that the DMK Government in Tamil Nadu be dismissed.”

There is more. When the AIADMK was allied with the BJP in the Union Government, senior BJP leader Yashwant Sinha was Finance Minister. In his book *Confessions of a Swadeshi Reformer*, he wrote on page 226:

“After my morning engagements in Chennai ended, I went to Jayalalithaa's house. Fortunately, no photographers were waiting there. I entered the room where she was, spoke for a while, and then we had lunch. Only three of us ate: Jayalalithaa, Dilip Ray, the Minister of State for Coal, and I. The food was good. As I left, Jayalalithaa gave me an envelope. When I opened it later, it contained notes about her income-tax cases.

“A few days later, I met the Prime Minister and told him about my talks with Jayalalithaa in Chennai. But I forgot to mention the envelope she had given me. The Prime Minister, Vajpayee, then asked, ‘What was in the envelope Jayalalithaa gave you?’ I was startled. I realised that when the Prime Minister wished to know what was happening in the country, he could do so.”
That is what former Union Finance Minister Yashwant Sinha recorded in his own book.

This occurred in 1998, when the AIADMK was part of an alliance with the BJP. It too concerned the income-tax case. Yashwant Sinha himself wrote that Jayalalithaa invited the Union Finance Minister to her home, served him lunch and gave him an envelope containing a request.

What is the position now? When the Prime Minister previously visited Tamil Nadu, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa did not go to receive him. Now she goes to the airport to welcome and see him off, and speaks with him privately for fifteen minutes. Only afterwards does the Income Tax Department inform the Egmore court that an application has been submitted to settle the matter and is under consideration. Department counsel tells reporters that he has no objection.

After this case travelled for seventeen years, Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and Sasi Enterprises applied to the Director General of Income Tax, saying they were prepared to pay the income tax due together with penalties under the Act. The Director General reportedly considered and accepted that application.

When the case came up on the first, the judge was reportedly absent because, for some reason, he had taken leave. Counsel for Jayalalithaa then filed a petition stating: “The Income Tax Department has accepted the settlement application submitted by Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and Sasi Enterprises. Sasi Enterprises has consequently paid Rs 75,33,330 with penalty for 1990-91 and Rs 65,67,872 for 1992-93. Jayalalithaa has paid Rs 30,83,887 and Sasikala Rs 28,07,972. In all, Rs 1,44,43,061 has been paid. The Income Tax Department has not issued its final order. Jayalalithaa and Sasikala therefore need not appear in person, and the hearing should be postponed for two weeks.” The case was adjourned accordingly.
The newspapers have written that this case is about to end. At this moment, several questions about it arise for us and for the people of the country.

Who is responsible for the many hours spent on this case over seventeen years by different courts, judges, Governments, lawyers and court officials? Is a fine the only punishment for those who wasted all their time? If people with money pay it, are all their offences forgotten? Had this amount, now paid with penalty, been paid seventeen years ago, would the labour of so many people not have been spared? What has become of the Supreme Court judges' earlier observations about Jayalalithaa's side? How much time did journalists spend on this case?

When the same BJP governed at the Centre in 1998, newspapers reported an effort to have this income-tax case withdrawn. Is it wrong if many believe that a similar step has been taken now? Jayalalithaa's side filed successive discharge petitions in the lower court, High Court and Supreme Court. Could they not instead have told the Income Tax Department at that very time that they would pay the penalty?

Though the judge repeatedly insisted that Jayalalithaa appear in court, she did not. The DMDK leader wrote to the President and Prime Minister asking whether an ordinary Indian citizen could have acted in this way and whether the Income Tax Department should accept Jayalalithaa's request after so many years. What became of those letters? When the courts rejected her discharge request in 2006, could Jayalalithaa not have applied then to settle with the Department? Did she refrain only because no Government at the Centre was then willing to heed her request?

If Jayalalithaa's side has now paid the penalty, does that not mean they have admitted the offence? If anyone else conducted a case for seventeen years and, just as judgment was about to be delivered, offered to pay the penalty because there was no other escape, would the Income Tax Department accept it?

Lawyers say that if Jayalalithaa had not settled and the court had imposed punishment, tax evasion exceeding Rs 1 lakh would attract, besides a fine, rigorous imprisonment for a minimum of six months and a maximum of seven years. Some say Jayalalithaa's side approached those who had to be approached and settled with the Income Tax Department precisely to escape that punishment.
However it may be, a doubt has arisen in everyone's mind: has a request rejected when Vajpayee was Prime Minister now been accepted under Prime Minister Modi?
With affection,
M.K.
4 December 2014
